transcript.text: Thank you.
Hi, Maricela.
Oh, sorry, Maricela.
Is that how I say it?
Yes.
Yes, that's right.
Hi.
Nice to meet you.
Nice to meet you, Bridget.
I'm just starting my day.
So I had a crazy weekend yesterday.
Well, it was starting calm, but then my fridge and my freezer broke.
So I spent the whole day, like everything like melted and went off.
Yes, that happened to us on December 25th, the last year.
Okay, I can't complain.
Yes.
Remind me where you are based.
I'm based in Costa Rica.
Oh, nice.
Okay, cool.
In the capital or where do you live?
Nearby.
I'm like 20 minutes away from San Jose.
That is the capital.
Awesome.
I've never been, but I would love to go.
Oh, you should come down here.
There are so many beautiful places, you know.
No, the beaches look amazing.
Yes, yes.
Awesome.
I live in Vancouver in Canada.
I guess cold?
Yeah, if not, it's warm as Costa Rica.
That's for sure.
But, uh, it's,
It's okay in the summer.
Awesome.
Well, I'm excited to meet you.
So I'll give you a little bit of info on me and the company.
And then we'll probably ask a few questions about your background and what you're looking for and all that.
So I just started at GrowthX like two and half months ago.
I run operations.
So my team handles recruiting, HR, finance, legal, IT.
So everything like in-house.
And then the rest of the company, I can give you sort of like an info, like sort of a high level overview of the company.
Today, it really looks like a content agency.
So what we're doing is we're helping different customers and brands to produce a bunch of content and also help them with their SEO so that they get like more traffic.
Traffic to their website as well, through different avenues.
And so client-facing looks like an agency, but we're building a bunch of AI workflows on the back end, which help us produce that volume of content and make sure that it's high quality.
And that way we can serve more and more customers without having to hire a one-to-one ratio of people forever.
So the idea is that we can supplement a lot of the work a writer would do with these AI workflows.
And then the team is just kind of helping the workflows create what it needs and checking it and make sure there's a quality check.
And additionally, providing more strategy behind it as well.
So obviously the AI needs to be prompted, and the more strategy and unique kind of prompts we can provide, the more unique content we can also produce.
So that's sort of the idea.
of the idea.
The is very new.
It's only, like, less than a year old.
Essentially, started last summer.
We have about 40 customers today, mostly U.S.-based customers, and we're at, like, $7 million in annual revenue.
So it's growing really fast, and then the team is about, we're actually at almost, like, 45 people right now, and we're spread all over the world.
Like, the most concentrated areas are the U.S., and we have, like, five people in Canada.
But other than that, there's kind of, like, one or two or three in a bunch of different countries all over the place.
That's great.
Yeah, it's really cool.
Lots of different cultures.
And then we all just try, at the very least, everyone usually overlaps, like, morning Pacific time.
Because we have people, like, in India or in...
So Kenya or Eastern Europe, right?
So they work into the evening their time.
But in the leadership team, so there's six of us, we are all on the West Coast.
So we all work Pacific hours.
Okay, that's great, because I'm just one hour ahead of you.
Oh, perfect.
Okay, I was gonna ask you that.
So yeah, so we generally work Pacific hours.
And then everybody, well, most people on the leadership team also have families.
So it's, everybody's usually kind of, you know, it's quiet, starting around five ish, I would say, it's quieter five to maybe eight.
And then a couple people will come back online, like do a little bit of work, but there's no like, you know, expectation to like respond late at night or anything like that.
So what I've been doing, I'm working with the near agency is I'm trying to find assistance for actually everybody on the team, on the leadership team, though, my
The priority one is to find an EA for the CEO.
So he has an EA right now and she's amazing.
She actually does like way more than just EA stuff.
She's been helping a lot with sales and invoicing and finances and all of that.
She's actually going to transition and support our other co-founder, who's our CTO.
And she has a lot of context on customers.
So it makes sense that she'll work with him and they're building out some different programs and stuff to support our team.
So that means I'm looking for a new EA for our CEO.
And the other reason that really came up is because one is he's in person a lot these days.

We have a new office that we have not moved into, but he's going to he's just doing a lot more volume of like in-person meetings that just requires like I feel like requires like an extra level of coordination and figuring out, you know, where.
And then the second thing is that he'd like to spend more time.
So he has quite a big following on LinkedIn.
And he tries to do like at least one post or two posts every week.
And when he does that, he gets like a ton of messages and requests and all that.
So he not only has like the usual email volume, he also has Slack, which is crazy because he's in all of our customer channels.
And then he's got his LinkedIn.
So he'll post some posts about our company and then he'll get like 50 messages.
And maybe 40 are just random that we don't need.
But maybe 10 are like investors or customers.
It's all buried in there.
So like how to know there's a lot of noise and then how to know where he should focus.
And just like obviously the goal is just like make his life easier so he can focus more on, you know, work.
The top priorities.
So, yeah, that's sort of a little bit of background.
things are moving quickly.
He happens to be in, like, he does a lot of sales calls because we don't really have a sales team.
He does a lot of customer calls because all the customers are pretty new.
Like, he does a lot of investor calls because the business is new and, like, hot and good revenue.
So, he's just, like, all over the map a little bit.
So, I'm trying to find him a partner who can really help just, like, organize his life better.
And so, just so he doesn't, like, I think he gets overwhelmed with life.
Yes, of course.
And I love that you mentioned a partner because, personally, for me, an executive assistant is not just an employee.
It has to be exactly what you said.
And a strategic partner that is not going to be just sitting down, going over a list of tasks, and that's it, is going to anticipate what.
And to be able to understand this, that you are going to manage in the time of the CEO in this case.
But I mean, time is revenue, know, time is also needed to think, recharge, and to be able to keep moving forward.
Yes.
Yeah, exactly.
And yeah, so and then thought partner too, right?

Like there's lots of times where it's just like need someone to like bounce an idea off of or, you know, I feel like so I've also been I've been working as a chief of staff for the last like, more than three years, like at my old company as well.
So I've also worked right for the CEO and like a lot of this stuff.
I've been lucky to work with some awesome years.
But it's a lot of the time, it's just kind of like, okay, you look at the week ahead or the weeks ahead.
And it's like this, you just know it looks crazy.
Like this is this is going to be it.
This is a recipe.
So then it's like, what can we do ahead of time to set it up for success better?
And so I find I end up working really closely with an EA as well to help provide additional context of which meetings are important or which ones can be skipped.
Exactly.
Exactly.
Prioritize.
Yeah.

then just while we're at it, so the other, so then I'm going to look for four other EAs for the other, like one is a sales leader, one is like marketing leader, and then one is, his title is chief content officer, but he really runs like the whole agency side.
So it's a big staff.
So it's lots of customer meetings, customer processes.
And then lastly will be one for myself, though I'm a little bit unsure still what I expect.
I spend a lot of time doing HR, so I'm also just toying with the idea of hiring sort of more of an HR help.
But anyway, just to give you context of the spectrum of things we're looking for, in case one of those sounds more interesting as well.
But anyway, I'd love to hear about your background and what you've been doing.
Well, it all started when I was very young, like 20 years.
I actually was born and raised in El Salvador, that is nearby.
So I started working there with this virtual assistant agency, and it was pretty innovative because it was like 2017.
So it was...
Yeah, like pre-COVID as well.
It was 2000...
Wait a minute.
It was a long time.
So everybody was like, what are you talking about?
You're not working in a call center.
So what do you do?
So that's like the base, like my foundation.
I'm very thankful for that experience because I get to work with up to five managers, CEO, depending on the level of task.
There was this one that it was like a full-time customer.
Others were like booked for two hours, one hour.
For some of them, I would just forward texts that they receive in their cell phones.
I mean, it was a different kind of task that helped me then.
It was my foundation.
So after that, in 2010, I started to work directly with the owner of this real estate company that it was based in Orlando, Florida.
I worked there for eight years and I started working basically like the sales agent.
I would do cold calling and BS scripts.
I would do prospecting of new possible buyers or seller of properties.
And then,
And I moved more into the admin side of things.
I was handling the company's email, any client's requests.
I was in charge of posting listings into the MLS or different real estate websites.
I was also doing a little bit of graphic design in Canva, Adobe Photoshop, retouching photos, creating posts, I mean, the posters for the open houses, ordering supplies, all admin.
And my last position there was transaction coordinator.
So I was basically in charge of all the paperwork related to the buy of the sale of a property.
And also meeting, contacting different, the real estate brokers, the loan officer, the banks.
So it was a lot of different positions, but I'm also thankful because I get to learn a lot.
Yeah.
And then my last position was at this insurance agency.
Also located in Orlando, Florida.
And I worked with them for seven years.
So as you can see, I'm used to building like long-lasting relationships with my peers.
And personally, it's something that I'm very proud of because I feel that I get to earn that trust.
And that's the best way for me to show you the quality of my work.
And at my last position here in this insurance agency, I started working as basically just had to task.
And it was handling the main company's email and creating tickets in Hotspot.
And I was also in charge of creating this specific report that was called the COI, that it was a request from certain insurance companies.
And then I slowly began transition working with the CEO.
And I was in charge of his email, answering on his behalf, managing his schedule.
It was a very complex schedule.
You know, sometimes I would have to book five calls throughout the day with 15-minute breaks.
I was in charge of planning his travels, booking events, at the same time contacting vendors that they were going to be participating in these events.
And I was doing all of this at the same time that I was still in my last position, working with the company's email, creation of tickets, maintaining the CRM.
So what I'm trying to say is that your company is something that really appeals to me because, like you mentioned, it's something that is constantly changing right now.
And for me, I was blessed enough because when I started working there at this insurance agency, they were, they had two offices and now they are around 10 offices.
Some of them are in Florida, some of them.
In Atlanta, Georgia.
So it was a very changing environment.
A lot of transitions from one software to the other.
A lot of different processes.
One week we were doing this specific task this way, and then we need to change it.
For me, it is very exciting to work that way.
I mean, I know it sounds a little bit chaotic, but it helps you stay sharp, you know, be present, being in the moment.
And like I was telling you before, anticipate needs.
You mentioned, you know, you're going to see the week and it's going to be, oh my God.
That happened to me with my CEO calendar, you know, when I see like too many different colors on the calendar meant that, I mean, there was something that, I mean, changes, last minute changes.
There was this time that we need to reschedule the whole week because an event.
Came in a last-minute notice.
Yeah.
I was there contacting clients, changing meetings, or making a 30-minute long meeting, a 15-minute meeting now.
So, like, always trying to stay calm, focus, and find a solution, you know?
Yes.
So basically, that has been my career so far.
And what do you do?

So let's say when you first start, how do you begin to learn and, like, download information to be able to know of, like, you know, I'm just thinking of random examples, but a LinkedIn message or an email comes in that's, you know, how do you know if it's important or they want to, you know, I want to meet with Marcel, but it's like, how do you start picking up all the context to know how to?
For me, it is having a constant open communication.
you.

you.
Thank you.
In this case, my CEO.
That's how I did it before in my last position.
I would have Monday meetings at the beginning of the day, also Friday meetings at the end of the day, constant communication like, hey, Maricela, I'm waiting for an email from this customer.
As soon as you see it, you have to let me know.
And you will see me chasing my CEO via email, via text, phone call, all the places possible, just to make sure that he's not going to miss this potential client meeting or something like that.
So for me, it's always constant communication, not only with the CEO, but also with the rest of the team, because I'm not working just by myself.
I'm becoming a part of your team and we need to know where everyone is, you know, and that's the best way for me to organize my priorities, understand what comes first, what can wait.
And then, personally, the way that I work is via time blocking.
I like to either divide my day by hours or by blocks, like in the beginning, at the middle of the day, and at the end of the day.
Yeah, which I think we should, which I think is probably best for everybody and even CEOs.

I know it's so hard because like, yes, meeting externally, there's, you know, but yeah, the more we can batch things are like Tuesday focus day and Wednesdays and yesterday or, you know, things like that.
Yeah, for example, with my CEO, there were these, not every week, but most of the time we have like these administrative days where he will block a whole day just for meetings with the internal team.
Myself, sales team, the other, the VPs, etc.
So it would be just one day, meetings back to back to be able to.
To touch everyone, you know.
Yeah.
Awesome.
What's, do you have an example of like a project that was, what's the word I want to use?
Like more complicated than normal or something and that was kind of outside of your.
Yes.
Yes.
Like, I mean, like I mentioned to you before, they were transitioning all the time.
They were improving processes.
So we were using these, these CRM call matrix.
It is an insurance, insurance CRM.
And they were trying to divide their clients to the VIPs, to the, the ones that they were in VIPs.
So they were, they were creating tax, you know, so the people that we had home and auto insurance, those are the ones that were going to be VIPs.
But, you know, sometimes processes don't turn out as well as we thought.
But, and.
They do this export of this sheet with all the missing tags.
And there were like 4,000 people that didn't have this tag.
And they asked me if I wanted to help them with this project.
I sat down and I said, I just cannot do it right now because I'm still in charge of all the other daily tasks.
So my solution was I'm going to be staying after work, but in order to be able to deliver this on time, we will need extra hands.
So I found another VA, you could say, that was coming to my home office and was working under my supervision.
So that's the way that, that's the best way that I found.
Well, I, I have many friends that used to work with me in this, in this field, you know, they were not with, the person that was with me, it wasn't part.
Full-time.
She would come like two, three hours, depending.
But, you know, the best way for me was to put some extra hands on the team.
And after that, from time to time, when another project like this would come, that's the way that we would be working.
So I really love that example because it's not healthy or, like, productive when you just, one, either overwhelm yourself and then you get paralyzed.
I don't know what to do.
I don't have, how am I going to find all these hours?
But then also sometimes the reality is you just, you will never find those hours.
And you just need to pick a new solution, which in that case was just, like, hire someone.
Hire a freelancer on up to work.
Just hire someone to help you.
And, like, yes, it takes a little bit of extra effort because you have to show them what to do.
But weigh that out and maybe long-term, you know, it's easier.
So that's a good example.
Like, it's kind of being.
Being resourceful is what I was, what was the summary.
Yeah, basically.
And I understand that it was a better repetitive task.
Yeah.
But I mean, the important for me was that that other person was going to deliver the same quality of my work.
So the best thing, I mean, the best solution that I found was, no, I just, I want you to be here in my home office for this time, the very first project.
Then we can, you can work.
Then you know how it works.
Yeah, that's a great, that's so true.
Because like, at the end of the day, it's still your responsibility.
Exactly.
delegate it.
Yeah.
Exactly.
Exactly.
Yes.
One more question is, I love asking this to people.
So what is some feedback you've received recently, if possible?
Like one positive feedback, maybe from your old manager or something.
And also one constructive feedback, that's, you know, something to work on.
Yeah.
Well, when I started working with my CEO schedule, there was a lot of, it was a learning curve.
It wasn't easy at the beginning because, like I said, it was a very busy schedule.
So I would say that that is where I get my constructive feedback.
Like, hey, I know you are, sometimes you want to go fast, but it's better to slow down and, you know, check the things even three times if you need to.
My CEO was very specific when creating a calendar invite, for example, because he would love to use the time in the time zone that that meeting is going to happen.
Whether if it was a call, he would add on the title of the call invite.
If it was Zoom or an in-person meeting, the name and the company name of the person that he was meeting and the phone number, just in case he needed to click on.
And just call.
So at the very beginning, I was missing all of these details because on my mind, it was like, I have to book this meeting now and I have to do it quick.
And he said, I understand that you want to do the things quickly because sometimes things can change from one minute to the other.
But I need for you to follow my process.
So that would be the constructive feedback.
And the positive feedback was with the example that I gave you.
They were very impressed by that solution, especially because the solution worked.
And we say that way for a long time.
They will hand out projects like this, spreadsheets.
There were missing information and they knew that I had this person with me.
So that was very impressive for them, too.
Nice.
And so on the constructive piece, how do you, I guess.
Did you, using that feedback, did you incorporate it into other areas of your work as well?
Yes.

I mean, working as an executive assistant is very important for you to be detail-oriented and to be able to master that balance between being detail-oriented, slowing down, but at the same time doing the things now.
So it's like, ironically, but you have to make it work.
So yeah, I mean, I was like, yes, I just, I'm not just going to hit the send button in an email.
I will re-read it, make sure that I was telling the story, you know, basically trying to slow down to speed up.
Yeah.

I think another thing that could, would help, and you may, you could probably do this is like, templates and like things where you can just like copy, you know, if you do, if it's like, yeah, like how can you make it faster every time?
Even if it is, you know, yeah, you don't want to go too fast and just like do it wrong.
But then how can you make it faster?
Maybe you have like a cheat sheet of like, yes, definitely.
I would have like this stuff.

How do you call a quick note here in my, in my, and the most, yeah, I mean, for the calendar invites, I would have like that template for the email confirmations because we would send out, I would send out 24 to 48 hours before of that meeting an email confirmation, just making sure that everything was on track.
I also had that template in place in this Word document.
So I made sure to copy paste and then just add the specific details.
Yes.
Awesome.

I do have a 930, but any questions for me?

Like, first of I really love chatting with you and I love your style and your communication is really great.
So I think there will 100% be a fit on the team.

just want to check if it will be with Marcel, CEO, or if it makes more sense to be with Matthew, who's our chief content officer, who has, you a team of 40 people and there's a lot going on there too.

there's kind of options of we have five execs who need an VA.

Yes.
